videos.title: YEREVAN is NOT What You Think. 🇦🇲😮‍💨
videos.description:
YEREVAN, ARMENIA is so much more than just another capital. Most people only know 1 or 2 things about Armenia, and neither are very useful in understanding what it's actually like to spend time in the city today.

I enjoyed Yerevan so much that I actually ended up extending my stay. Between the incredible people, the art, the culture, and the food, there was so much for me to consistently be entertained.

The country has faced a great deal of adversity over the years, most notably through the Armenian Genocide. But the best part of my visit was seeing how the people are so hospitable to foreigners, even despite everything that they've gone through in the past. It was super inspiring and made me love the city even more.

Are you from Armenia? Yerevan? I can't wait to hear what you think. Let me know YOUR favorite activities and food in the comments, and be sure to LIKE, SUBSCRIBE, and click the BELL so you never miss a chance to GET LOST with me!

***

Looking for some tours? I have some great ones for you to check out:

MONASTERIES TOUR: https://tinyurl.com/yck9ymwa
PARAGLIDING: https://tinyurl.com/3dp66kmc
PAGAN TEMPLE: https://tinyurl.com/mr38fbyk

These 3 Links are Viator Affiliate Links which help me get paid to keep travelin'!

***

"Get Lost!" is my chance to connect with fellow travelers. I've been to 37 countries and my dream is to set foot on every nation on the planet. I hope that my channel can bring you some joy, entertainment, and laughter as we build our travel & food itineraries together.

I'm always looking to connect with like-minded individuals! I hope you'll send me your favorite travel channels so we can build a community together right here on Youtube. I'd love to hear what you're loving, what you'd like to see me do next, or where you're itching for more travel information from, in every city and every nation on planet Earth.

Find me on Instagram @j.m.payne

00:00 INTRO
00:30 THE PEOPLE
01:44 THE HISTORY
03:26 THE ART
05:34 THE CULTURE
06:50 EYES OPENED
videos.transcript: if you're from the West, you probably only know two things about Armenia: one that there was a terrible genocide there in the early 20th century, and two that it's the country where the Kardashians claim their Heritage. Now both of these things are true, but neither will tell you very much about what it's like to be in Armenia right now. I spent almost a week there, and every single day I learned a little bit more about the nation and its people, and I'll tell you right now that it's it's nothing like you might think. This is Yerevan, the capital city of Armenia. A country is nothing if not its people, and I have to say that I was genuinely shocked by my experiences with the locals in Yerevan. Now for about 2 weeks beforehand I had been based out of Tbilisi, Georgia. Since there is no bus or train, you can either fly, or you can take a $16 minivan ride between the two cities. Yeah, minivan. Like uh, like the ones that soccer moms use. My first experience with an Armenian was with my minivan driver. He didn't speak any English at all, but he went out of his way to make sure that we all had water whenever we needed it, and he used the Google translate on his phone to invite us to come visit him uh after we arrived in Yerevan. Great first impression. The next day, as I usually do on my first day, I took a free walking tour. And for the next 4 hours the guide showed me just how Scrappy Armenians can be. Even though he was only working for tips, this guy took no s***. You can really tell that he takes his role as sort of an ambassador of his Nation seriously. Over the course of the tour, he really described to us the history of Armenia in detail, and when you start to really learn about the constant adversity that they have faced ,and that they continue to face, you can quickly understand why he would be so passionate about building these sorts of bridges. On that first day everyone I met was so open and forthcoming and passionate, it was really refreshing. It blows my mind that a genocide that killed somewhere between 600,000 and 1 and a half million people has gone largely ignored, and in some cases totally unrecognized on a global scale. For me personally, I had heard the phrase "Armenian Genocide", but I didn't really have any context or understanding of what that meant. That is, until I spent an afternoon at the Armenian Genocide Memorial and Museum. It is extremely educational and profound if you allow it to be. Now I'm sure that they realize just how undereducated foreigners are on this subject, and so the museum largely features these walls of text that just inform you on every single possible detail. And so you do have to go with the intention to learn, it's heavy material, but you should go in with the intention to learn. Give yourself an afternoon. It's totally free, and it's an immensely valuable cultural and historical experience. Now, of course the history of Armenia is widespread and it goes far beyond just this tragic genocide. They were actually the first nation in history to adopt Christianity as the state religion, and if you want to dig deeper you can find all sort of historical and religious artifacts at the central History Museum in Republic Square. My favorite Historical Museum in Yerevan was the Matenadaran which houses all these historical manuscripts from many different countries in many different languages from across history. Wait what is that? It's a shame that fancy writing as an art form has been mostly lost to time, with new and continued advancements in technology. But I see you calligraphers, keep calligraph-calligraphizing? calligrap-- Keep doing calligraphy, calligraphers. Armenia was formerly a part of the Soviet Union, and because of that when I imagined visiting Armenia, I sort of imagined um a lot of buildings and architecture in that very brutalist style. And it does have that, in some ways, but I quickly learned two things. Firstly, I learned that the brutalist style can actually be kind of pretty. I found that especially true at one of the most famous sites of Armenia, the Cascades. It is this huge like brutalist style sort of Stairway, and I think it's gorgeous. And secondly I learned that Yerevan has a true and genuine appreciation for the arts. Especially sculpture. I mean I've never been anywhere that was so heavily covered in sculpture. The Cascade complex houses the Cafesjian center, which is primarily dedicated to sculpture as an art form. The foot of the Cascades has sculptures from so many different famous artists including three from one of my personal favorites Fernando Botero. I mean just take a look at this guy. And also this girl. And this naked guy. But mostly take a look at this guy. On a clear day at the top of the Cascades you're just going to get this incredible view of Mount Ararat, and honestly one of the best parts, for me at least, is that you don't even have to walk up all the stairs. There's these escalators inside that are going to take you all the way up to the top. Oerfect for us Americans, right? Because uh we don't like to exerc--- Speaking of an appreciation for the Arts, The Armenian national gallery has quickly become one of my favorite museums in the world. There aren't a ton of, you know, super famous pieces here or anything, but what I really enjoyed was the way that it was all curated. Different rooms are all painted in different colors in order to emphasize you know different emotions and to utilize color theory and all that. On the outside of the building, there are these gorgeous fountains that are just putting on a show all day long. But if you're lucky enough to visit at night, it's going to look like this. The night that I went there was actually sort of a special different light show taking place. Instead of with the fountains, it utilized projections of light onto the museum itself. It was actually pretty incredible. I'll just I'll let you watch for a moment for yourself. [Music] The most eye-opening thing about visiting Yerevan was realizing that I sort of live my life in a way that's very similar-- "shimilar" I sort of live my life very similarly to the way that the Armenians do. The work day often times starts a little bit later in Armenia, often times from 10:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. because the people just love being out at night. Not necessarily even partying per se, but just hanging out, and socializing, and exploring later into the evening. Aside from even just bars, you'll see these like gamer clubs all over the place which were so cool. There's tons of jazz music all over town. So there really is something at night for pretty much everyone. As it is, I also love the night. I'm a night owl. I also found out that Armenia is the only country in the world to make chess a mandatory subject in school, just like math or science. As it is, I also love --ow I bit my tongue-- as it is, I also love chess. Challenge me sometime on chess.com. I'll find a way to blunder my queen in a way that you never imagined possible. The local bread is called lavash, and it's so important to the people that it's actually been added to unesco's intangible cultural heritage list. As it is, I also love and Revere bread in potentially unhealthy ways. We're the same, I honestly I get these people. Give me some nighttime bread and chess and I'm good. I'm thriving. Things in the caucuses are complicated at best. Georgia, Armenia, and Azerbaijan are constantly either fighting against each other or with or against Russia. Couple that with historical issues such as the Armenian Genocide, which was perpetuated by present day Turkey, and Armenia sort of finds itself surrounded by Conflict at all times. Internally, the politics are complicated as a result of all of these things. Understandably. I I mean the people just want to ensure their own safety, which has been in Jeopardy many times over the years. Why do I bring all this up? Well because before visiting, it was my guess that Armenians would be grumpy and unwelcoming and unfriendly to foreigners. It wasn't based on anything I ever personally experienced, it's just that their history is so filled with strife and conflict that you would almost understand if they were maybe a little bit less welcoming to foreigners. But it honestly could not have been more the opposite. I felt actively welcomed. Yerevan is a beautiful city, with lovely people, gorgeous scenery, amazing art and culture, and a unique history. It is certainly a bit of a Hidden Gem, at least when comes to American tourism, but Yerevan has quickly become one of my favorite cities that I have ever visited, and I will absolutely be telling anyone who will listen, including you, to go as soon as you can.

safetyNotes: Because uh we don't like to exerc--- Speaking of an appreciation for the Arts, The Armenian national gallery has quickly become one of my favorite museums in the world
recommendations: But the best part of my visit was seeing how the people are so hospitable to foreigners, even despite everything that they've gone through in the past

videos.title: 20 Travel Tips for Yerevan Armenia: Things to know before you go
videos.description:
All photographs and video clips in this video are from my vacation.

Video edited by @JessicaShaw 

A few small corrections.

* Google translate does support Armenian for text but not voice. It does do voice translation for Russian.
* You can also fly to Armenia via Qatar and may be able to have fewer connections
* There are more options for getting to Armenia from the US via Europe than I experienced out of Denver. From your location you may find better options. When I booked my trip no matter what I did it always took me in and out of Yerevan to Europe at 4am.
* If you ask a waiter in a restaurant to add a tip to the bill they will. But it's not automatic like it is in the US


--20 Travel Tips I wish I knew before visiting Yerevan, Armenia: 

-Bring treats for Yerevan street dogs
-Don’t rent a car
-Walk around Yerevan in safety
-Flashing police lights do not mean that you are in trouble. Getting yelled at in Armenian over a loudspeaker might mean that you are though
-Bring both cash and credit cards. American Express is not widely accepted
Avoid getting large bills (10,000 or 20,000 drams) when exchanging currency
-Streetlights have a countdown timer telling you how long you have to cross or how long you have to wait
-Use Google translate when needed, don’t expect many people to speak English
-Don’t bother to smile or nod at strangers on the street. They won’t
-Don’t try to fly to Armenia in one long travel day. It’s just too much
-Give yourself plenty of time for your connecting flights
-Try to upgrade to business class on your flight out 
-Do walk up the cascade complex and to the genocide memorial and sculpture gardens
-Get Google Fi for your travel cellphone
-Go on a tour to Garni Temple, Symphony of Stones, and Gerhardt Monastery
-If you have the fitness level, take a hiking tour; perhaps to the south peak of Mount Aragats
-Get an ice cream or smoothie at any one of the hundreds of small shops
-Explore the parks and especially see the water fountain shows at night
-Souvenir shopping at Vernissage
-Enjoy the nightlife in Yerevan especially the open air bars and cafes with live music near Republic Square and the Opera House

safetyNotes: Avoid getting large bills (10,000 or 20,000 drams) when exchanging currency

videos.title: The ARMENIA Nobody Prepared Me For | YEREVAN First Impressions 🇦🇲
videos.description:
🇦🇲 FIRST STEPS IN YEREVAN – FROM AIRPORT ARRIVAL TO GETTING LOST IN A CITY THAT FEELS LIKE HOME 🇦🇲 | BUS 201, VIVA SIM CARD & MY FIRST WALK THROUGH YEREVAN (ARMENIA) 🇦🇲

Some cities don’t just sit on a map; they live in you long before you arrive. For Armenians abroad in the US, UK, Canada and across Europe, this is more than an airport vlog – it is that first breath of air in Yerevan (Երևան / Ереван), the first Armenian sign you read in real life and the shock of finally seeing real Armenian streets, faces and voices again.

The journey begins inside Zvartnots International Airport (Զվարթնոց միջազգային օդանավակայան / Международный аэропорт Звартноц). I step off the plane, grab my backpack and go straight into “arrival mode”: exchanging euros into Armenian dram, feeling local banknotes in my hands and walking to the Viva-MTS shop (Վիվա-ՄՏՍ / Вива-МТС) for a SIM card with unlimited internet so I can stay connected from the very first minute.

Outside the airport, I cross to the stop for Bus 201, the microbus that links Zvartnots with the city center. The stop is close to arrivals and full of familiar travel details, and when the microbus arrives I squeeze inside, find a seat and watch the “real Yerevan” roll by – neighborhoods outside the center, apartment blocks, corner shops, hills and traffic.

The bus drops me on Mashtots Avenue (Մեսրոպ Մաշթոցի պողոտա / Проспект Маштоца), one of the city’s main arteries. My first impression hits quickly as I walk toward my hotel: wide streets, stone buildings, trees, people crossing in every direction, cafés, bakeries, restaurants and small supermarkets where life moves calmly around me.

Soon I reach Pushkin Street (Պուշկին փողոց / Улица Пушкина) and convince myself the hotel must be close – but it isn’t. I meet kindness when a gentleman points me in the right direction and later another group of locals calls the hotel to get clearer directions.

I wander through side streets and quiet courtyards, learning the shape of Yerevan one wrong turn at a time until I finally spot the entrance to my hotel. Inside, the person at reception greets me with a calm smile, helps with the check-in and makes everything feel relaxed and human, and the episode ends there at the front desk with that small but powerful feeling that you have arrived and you are safe, and a new chapter in Armenia (Հայաստան / Армения) is beginning.

🌍 JOIN ME AS I EXPLORE ARMENIA
Join me as I explore ARMENIA – starting from YEREVAN’s busy avenues and side streets and moving toward mountain towns, spiritual sites, forests and lakeside villages. Each episode visits markets, cafés and everyday neighborhoods that carry deep meaning for Armenians abroad and for travelers seeing the country for the first time.

Armenia is ideal for slow solo travel, long city walks, digital nomads and remote workers searching for a base with warmth, community and affordable living. If you are part of the Armenian diaspora and dream about spending a season in Yerevan, working from a café, speaking Armenian every morning and walking home under soft orange streetlights, this series is for you.

📌 PERFECT FOR
• Armenians abroad who miss ordinary Armenian days, familiar accents and neighborhood walks 🇦🇲
• Travelers who love honest first impressions, real-life city walks and everyday local transport
• Food lovers curious about traditional Armenian dishes, bakeries and family restaurants
• Remote workers and digital nomads looking for safe, welcoming, budget-friendly cities with real community

🛏️ Visiting or planning a trip to Armenia?
🇦🇲 Hotels in Armenia 👉 https://bit.ly/4ikArX7
🌍 Hotels worldwide 👉 https://bit.ly/4lcDpOk
🚗 Rent a car in Armenia 👉 https://bit.ly/43DGDn0
📱 Travel eSIM 👉 https://bit.ly/3IckAvR
🔥 73% OFF NordVPN 👉 https://bit.ly/4kiGX02
⚠️ Limited-time deals - book early to save
Affiliate links - I may earn a small commission at no extra cost to you.

🎒 ABOUT THIS SERIES
Roaming Mario in Armenia is for anyone who feels a connection to this country – whether you grew up here, heard family stories or discovered Armenia later through friends and community abroad. Each episode is a gentle love letter to everyday life: buses, supermarkets, corner cafés, side streets, hidden courtyards and long walks through neighborhoods that might feel like home again.

🎥 Filmed in 4K HDR - best watched on HDR devices.

00:00 Arriving At Yerevan Airport
00:19 Exchanging Money And First Moments In Armenia
00:38 Getting A Viva-MTS SIM Card With Unlimited Internet
01:54 Finding Bus 201 And Leaving Zvartnots Airport
03:05 The Real Yerevan (Armenia)
05:37 First Impressions On Mashtots Avenue
07:50 First Impressions of Yerevan (Armenia)
10:58 Lost Near Pushkin Street And Asking Locals For Help
12:00 Finally Finding The Hotel And Checking In

videos.title: TOP 10 Things to do in Yerevan, Armenia 2023!
videos.description:
TOP 10 Things to do in Yerevan, Armenia 2023! 

Yerevan, Armenia is an amazing place to visit in Armenia. If you want to know top 10 what to do in Yerevan or you need a travel guide, please keep watching

For number 6, we invite you to Explore the Vernissage Flea Market: This bustling open-air market showcases an eclectic array of handmade crafts, traditional Armenian art, vintage items, and souvenirs. It's a fantastic opportunity to immerse yourself in the local culture, interact with friendly artisans, and take home one-of-a-kind treasures that embody the rich heritage and creativity of Armenia.


Moving on to number 5, we have the Old Town: The narrow winding streets, charming architecture, and historic landmarks create an enchanting ambiance. Discover ancient churches, traditional houses, and artisan workshops that showcase the city's rich heritage. Immerse yourself in the vibrant local culture, savor Armenian delicacies, and experience the warmth of the locals, making it a truly unforgettable and culturally enriching experience.


At number 4, we recommend Visiting the Armenian Opera and Ballet Theatre: As one of the oldest opera houses in the world, it showcases a rich heritage of classical and contemporary performances. From mesmerizing ballets to soul-stirring operas, the theater's impeccable acoustics and grand architecture immerse visitors in an unforgettable artistic journey, highlighting the nation's deep appreciation for the performing arts.


At number 3, we highly recommend a visit to Cafesjian Center for the Arts: This architectural gem showcases a diverse collection of contemporary and modern art, both local and international. With its stunning setting in the Cascade complex, the center offers breathtaking views of Mount Ararat and a rich cultural experience through its exhibitions, making it a unique and inspiring destination in the heart of Armenia's capital.


Now, let's unveil number 2 on our list—Taste Armenian Cuisine: With a rich culinary heritage, the restaurant offers a delightful array of delectable food, from succulent kebabs to flavorful dolmas and baklava. Immerse yourself in the vibrant ambiance and warm hospitality, providing an unforgettable dining experience that celebrates the essence of Armenia's gastronomic culture.


Finally, securing the number 1 spot on our list is Erebuni Museum and Fortress: It allows you to explore the ancient origins of the city, with well-preserved artifacts and exhibits showcasing its rich history. The museum provides fascinating insights into the Urartian civilization and the founding of Yerevan, making it a must-visit destination for history enthusiasts and those seeking a deeper understanding of Armenia's cultural heritage.
tags: top 10 things to do in armenia

go the index go works exactly like uber you pick where you want to go where you want to get picked up and you can pay in cash we love apps like this because you know how much you're paying before you get into the taxi not all taxes around here have meters and you have to negotiate we were actually told by a local two pan cash there is an option to put your card information in but they said don't so I wouldn't either I think that's mostly just because everybody pays with cash here so they're just used to it maybe they don't trust cards as much I know perhaps the other day we used this taxi app at the airport through Viva MTS we got five gigabytes of data for about 2600 dram and that was a great deal you also get unlimited data for a ton of social media apps like YouTube Twitter Tick Tock all of that so you're pretty covered we briefly mentioned this when we were down in the Metro but having phone service has seriously just saved us it's really something you're going to want when you come here this may or may not be useful to some of you but as a female I do question what I am and am not allowed to wear in certain locations and here in Yerevan you are perfectly fine to wear a tank top and shorts so in case that helps [Music] so far we've showed you what to expect when it comes to the cost of lodging cost of food cost of transportation and which mode of transportation you may or may not want to take but last but not least let's talk about some sightseeing in and around this city [Music] you've already seen what Republic Square looks like which is the center of town but now we're going to take you to the Cascade complex I've heard that if you missed this in your van then you didn't really see the city Nathan says this is his favorite statue of all time no clue why he says it's the mustache it is the staircase Cascade is so much more than just a staircase it holds the Contemporary Art Museum and it can be considered the original decoration of the city and you don't have to climb the stairs to get to the top it's so unfold [Music] I couldn't find the escalator we could use the exercise it's okay [Music] we just realized this isn't even the top but just as far as the escalators took us got a little ways to go [Music] this is pretty amazing I really wish every city had something like this because we would go to the top all the time I love that it's so much more than just an Overlook of the city there is Art and storytelling the entire way I'm impressed another major recommendation in the city of Yerevan is the Armenian Genocide Museum and as far as I understand the admission is completely free in case anyone's wandering the escalator entrance is like way off to the side that's how we missed it you have to walk into the art museum yeah another Museum that you may be interested in visiting is the Museum of ancient manuscripts tickets for adults are about 1500 Gram it's also recommended that you pay extra for a guide then you just get the most out of your visit if you're interested in taking inexpensive and quick day trips outside of your van check out the Garney Temple and the gay guard Monastery we will not be visiting either because we actually have our own plans coming up which involves a several hour bus ride to the other side of Armenia if you want to see exactly where that bus ride takes us hit that subscribe button please and follow along and if you found this video useful at all please let us know in the comments below and if you think there's any vital information that we left out also let us know comment below help us out help others out this was what we learned in EuroVan we thought it would be useful to you guys but stay tuned for what's coming up next we're really excited to explore more of Armenia foreign

videos.description:
Thinking of traveling to Armenia? Before you pack your bags, watch this essential guide covering everything you need to know-from visas and accommodations to local currency, cash apps, SIM cards, food,
and travel tips. Whether you are a first-time visitor or a seasoned traveler, this video will help you plan your trip like a pro.

Subscribe to our channel - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCvo8wzjGrNvX_4O-f09kePA

We are a couple from Mumbai, India, passionate about travel, food, and exploring new cultures. Having traveled to around 20 countries and counting, we love documenting our adventures and sharing our experiences with you. From stunning landscapes to hidden gems, local cuisines, and travel tips, our channel is all about inspiring and helping fellow travelers. You can join us in our journey and share your thoughts in the comments below.
